title: C++ File Handling
content:
### File Handling

File handling in C++ is performed through the `<fstream>` library, which provides classes for reading from and writing to files.

---

### File Stream Classes

| Class | Description | Header |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| `ofstream` | Output file stream (write) | `<fstream>` |
| `ifstream` | Input file stream (read) | `<fstream>` |
| `fstream` | File stream (read/write) | `<fstream>` |

---

### Opening a File

```cpp
#include <fstream>

ofstream outFile("output.txt");       // Open for writing (destroys content)
ifstream inFile("input.txt");         // Open for reading
fstream file("data.txt", ios::app);   // Open in append mode
```

---

### File Modes

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `ios::in` | Open for reading |
| `ios::out` | Open for writing |
| `ios::app` | Append to end of file |
| `ios::ate` | Seek to end on open |
| `ios::binary` | Open in binary mode |
| `ios::trunc` | Truncate file if exists |

---

### Checking File Status

```cpp
ifstream file("data.txt");

if (file.is_open()) {
  cout << "File opened successfully";
}

if (file.good()) {
  cout << "No errors";
}

if (!file) {
  cout << "Error opening file";
}
```

---

### Closing a File

```cpp
file.close();  // Also called automatically when object goes out of scope
```

---

> **Key Point:** The file stream destructor automatically closes the file. However, explicitly calling `close()` is good practice, especially when you need to reopen the same stream object.

quiz.question: Which header provides file stream classes?
